S.label: Security
S.hint: How hardened you are. Fully breached at 0.

X.label: Stress
X.hint: Team pressure. Lower is better. Burnout at 100.

decisions.name: TRUST
decisions.meaning: Do exactly what the message or caller asks. Comply with the request.

decisions.name: VERIFY
decisions.meaning: Independently confirm through a second, trusted channel before acting.

decisions.name: REPORT
decisions.meaning: Flag it to IT / Security (report-phishing button, ticket, or security@).

decisions.name: IGNORE
decisions.meaning: Do nothing. Delete or dismiss it and move on.

decisions.name: ESCALATE
decisions.meaning: Hand the decision to a manager or security lead for approval.

scenarios.title: The Password Reset
scenarios.text: An urgent email “from Microsoft” warns your account will be locked in 24 hours. It tells you to click a link and re-enter your password now.

TRUST.text: You enter your password on the fake page. Credentials stolen — the attacker logs in.

VERIFY.text: You hover the link, see a strange domain, and open the real portal yourself. No harm done.

REPORT.text: You use ‘Report Phishing’. Security blocks the sender for the whole company.

IGNORE.text: You delete it. You’re safe, but colleagues still receive the same trap.

ESCALATE.text: Your manager confirms it’s fake. Minor delay, no harm.

scenarios.learn: Real providers never ask you to confirm a password through an email link — go to the site yourself, or report it.

scenarios.title: The Reuse Trap
scenarios.text: IT warns that a website where you used your work password has been breached. The same password also protects your work email.

TRUST.text: You assume it’s fine and change nothing. Attackers reuse the password and break into your email.

VERIFY.text: You confirm the alert is really from IT, then realise the risk. Good caution.

REPORT.text: You tell security, who force a reset before anyone gets in.

IGNORE.text: You move on. The reused password is quietly abused weeks later.

ESCALATE.text: You ask IT to reset everywhere and switch on MFA and a password manager.

scenarios.learn: One leaked password unlocks everything if you reuse it — use unique passwords and a password manager.

scenarios.title: Approve? Approve? Approve?
scenarios.text: Your phone buzzes with login-approval requests over and over at 11pm — sign-ins you never started. One more pops up.

TRUST.text: You tap Approve just to make it stop. The attacker is now inside your account.

VERIFY.text: You check the location and time, see it isn’t you, and deny it.

REPORT.text: You deny it and alert security. They lock the account and block the attacker.

IGNORE.text: You deny and silence your phone. The attack stops, but no one is warned.

ESCALATE.text: You call IT, who reset your credentials immediately.

scenarios.learn: Never approve a login prompt you didn’t start — repeated prompts are an attack. Deny and report.

scenarios.title: Scan to Pay
scenarios.text: A sticker on the office parking meter reads “Scan to pay / update card details.” The QR code opens a payment page.

TRUST.text: You scan and enter your card. The details are stolen on a fake page.

VERIFY.text: You use the meter’s official app instead of the sticker. Safe.

REPORT.text: You tell facilities/security. The fake sticker is removed and others are protected.

IGNORE.text: You ignore it and pay another way. No harm to you.

ESCALATE.text: Facilities investigate where the sticker came from.

scenarios.learn: A QR code hides where it really goes — don’t scan random ones in public; use the official app or site.

scenarios.title: The Bank Details Changed
scenarios.text: A known supplier emails a normal-looking invoice, with a note: “Please note our bank account has changed — pay the new account below.”

TRUST.text: You pay the new account. The money goes straight to a fraudster.

VERIFY.text: You call the supplier on the number you already have. The change is fake.

REPORT.text: You flag it to security and finance. The fraud is caught and others are warned.

IGNORE.text: You don’t pay at all. Awkward with a real supplier, but no money lost.

ESCALATE.text: Dual approval catches the mismatch before payment.

scenarios.learn: Always confirm changed bank details by calling a number you already trust — never the one on the invoice.

scenarios.title: Free Airport Wi-Fi
scenarios.text: At the airport you see “Airport_Free_WiFi” and “FREE Airport WiFi!!”. You need to send a client contract now.

TRUST.text: You join the open network and send it. Your traffic is snooped on a rogue hotspot.

VERIFY.text: You confirm the official network and use the company VPN. Safe send.

REPORT.text: Little to report here, but you note the suspicious twin network.

IGNORE.text: You wait and use your phone’s data instead. Safe, slight delay.

ESCALATE.text: You ask IT to confirm VPN access first.

scenarios.learn: Open Wi-Fi can be faked and snooped — use a VPN or your own mobile hotspot for anything sensitive.

scenarios.title: The Found Drive
scenarios.text: You find a USB stick in the car park labelled “Payroll 2025 — Confidential.” Curiosity says plug it in.

TRUST.text: You plug it in. Malware runs automatically and spreads onto the network.

VERIFY.text: There’s no safe way to ‘check’ it by plugging in — you treat it as suspicious.

REPORT.text: You hand it to IT, who examine it safely in isolation.

IGNORE.text: You bin it. Safe, but a possible drop attack goes unreported.

ESCALATE.text: IT investigate a possible ‘USB drop’ targeting the office.

scenarios.learn: ‘USB drop’ attacks bait your curiosity — never plug in a drive you found. Hand it to IT.

scenarios.title: The CEO’s Voice
scenarios.text: A call comes in. It sounds exactly like the CEO: “I’m in a meeting — move €40,000 to this account right now, and keep it quiet.”

TRUST.text: You make the transfer. The voice was AI-cloned; the funds are gone.

VERIFY.text: You call the CEO back on a known number (or use your code word). It’s fake.

REPORT.text: You flag the attempt to security. The team is warned about voice-clone fraud.

IGNORE.text: You hang up. Safe, but the attempt isn’t reported.

ESCALATE.text: You require the normal payment-approval process, which stops it.

scenarios.best: VERIFY (call back on a trusted number)
scenarios.learn: AI can clone a voice. Urgency + secrecy + money is the scam pattern — always verify on a known channel.

scenarios.title: The Helpful Post
scenarios.text: An intern proudly posts a first-day photo — their screen, their badge, and “Can’t believe how easy our new vendor portal login is!”

TRUST.text: The post stays up. Attackers harvest the details for a targeted phishing attack.

VERIFY.text: You check exactly what’s visible in the photo first.

REPORT.text: You quietly flag it; the post is taken down and friendly guidance is given.

IGNORE.text: The post stays public and the risk remains.

ESCALATE.text: You ask a manager to clarify the social-media policy for everyone.

scenarios.learn: Badges, screens and ‘behind the scenes’ posts leak details attackers use. Think before you post.

scenarios.title: Your Files Are Locked
scenarios.text: A red full-screen message appears: “All your files are encrypted. Pay $2,000 in crypto within 48 hours or lose everything.” A colleague says “just pay it!”

TRUST.text: You pay the ransom. There’s no guarantee of recovery — and you’re now a known payer.

VERIFY.text: You don’t negotiate alone — you get help before doing anything.

REPORT.text: You disconnect the device and call IT at once. The spread is contained; backups restore the files.

IGNORE.text: You keep working. The ransomware spreads to shared drives.

ESCALATE.text: Incident response and leadership coordinate a clean recovery — no payment.

scenarios.learn: Don’t pay and don’t panic — isolate the device and call IT. Good backups beat ransoms.

scenarios.title: WhatsApp from the CFO
scenarios.text: A WhatsApp from a new number shows your CFO’s photo: “Hi, stuck in a meeting — can you sort an urgent supplier payment? I’ll explain later.”

TRUST.text: You make the payment. It goes to a fraudster impersonating the CFO.

VERIFY.text: You call the CFO on their known number. They never sent it.

REPORT.text: You report the message; the team is warned and the number is blocked.

IGNORE.text: You don’t act on it. Safe.

ESCALATE.text: The standard approval process stops the rushed payment.

scenarios.learn: Executives don’t run urgent payments via WhatsApp from new numbers — verify on a channel you trust.

scenarios.title: Send Me Payroll
scenarios.text: A Microsoft Teams message from your manager’s account: “Hey, can you send me the payroll spreadsheet urgently? I’m boarding a flight.”

TRUST.text: You send the file. The account was hijacked — employee data is leaked.

VERIFY.text: You reach your manager another way and learn their account was compromised.

REPORT.text: You flag the account to IT, who secure it before more damage is done.

IGNORE.text: You don’t send it, but the hijacked account stays active and dangerous.

ESCALATE.text: Manager and security are looped in and the account is locked.

scenarios.learn: Even a trusted account can be hijacked — verify unusual data requests through another channel.

scenarios.title: Your Parcel Is Waiting
scenarios.text: SMS: “Your delivery failed. Pay a €1.99 redelivery fee here: [link].” You happen to be expecting a parcel.

TRUST.text: You pay the ‘fee.’ Your card and details are stolen, with recurring charges.

VERIFY.text: You check the courier’s official app — there’s no such fee.

REPORT.text: You report the SMS; the link is blocked for others too.

IGNORE.text: You delete it. Safe.

ESCALATE.text: Not much to escalate — but you warn a colleague.

scenarios.learn: Couriers don’t collect ‘fees’ via random SMS links — check the official tracking app instead.

scenarios.title: The Fake Executive
scenarios.text: A brand-new account using the CEO’s name and photo messages staff: “I need your mobile number for a quick confidential task.”

TRUST.text: You reply and help, and get drawn into a gift-card / transfer scam.

VERIFY.text: You confirm with the CEO’s real contact — the account is fake.

REPORT.text: You report the impersonation; the account is taken down and staff are warned.

IGNORE.text: You ignore it. Safe for you; others may still be targeted.

ESCALATE.text: Comms and security alert all staff to the fake account.

scenarios.learn: Impersonation accounts copy names and photos — verify identity before acting on ‘confidential’ asks.

scenarios.title: IT Support Calling
scenarios.text: A caller says: “This is IT — we’ve found a virus on your PC. I’ll walk you through installing a fix; just grant me remote access.”

TRUST.text: You install the ‘fix’ and grant access. The caller now controls your PC.

VERIFY.text: You hang up and call IT on the number you know. There’s no such ticket.

REPORT.text: You report the call so security can warn others.

IGNORE.text: You hang up and move on. Safe.

ESCALATE.text: IT investigate the spoofed support call.

scenarios.learn: Real IT won’t cold-call you for remote access — hang up and call the number you already know.

scenarios.title: A Quick Favour
scenarios.text: Email from “the boss”: “At your desk? I need €500 in gift cards for client gifts — send me the codes. In meetings, can’t talk.”

TRUST.text: You buy the cards and send the codes. The money is gone and unrecoverable.

VERIFY.text: You call the boss directly. They never asked for this.

REPORT.text: You report it; colleagues are warned about the same scam.

IGNORE.text: You don’t act. Safe.

ESCALATE.text: A manager confirms it’s a scam.

scenarios.learn: ‘Buy gift cards and send the codes’ is almost always a scam — verify any unusual money request.

scenarios.title: Hold the Door
scenarios.text: Someone in a delivery uniform, arms full of boxes, smiles at the secure door behind you: “Thanks — could you hold it? I’ve forgotten my badge.”

TRUST.text: You hold the door. An unauthorised stranger is now inside the building.

VERIFY.text: You politely point them to reception to sign in. They had no business there.

REPORT.text: You alert security, who check and escort them.

IGNORE.text: You let the door close behind you. Safe-ish, but no one is alerted.

ESCALATE.text: Reception verifies whether they have an appointment.

scenarios.learn: ‘Tailgating’ bypasses all your tech security — everyone badges in, no exceptions, however polite.

scenarios.title: There’s an App for That
scenarios.text: To work faster, a teammate suggests uploading the full client list to a free online tool they found to ‘clean it up.’

TRUST.text: You upload the data. The client list is now in the hands of an unknown third party.

VERIFY.text: You check with IT whether the tool is approved — it isn’t, so you find a safe option.

REPORT.text: You ask IT to vet it; they approve a safe alternative.

IGNORE.text: You stick with the approved process. No harm done.

ESCALATE.text: IT review and provide a sanctioned tool.

scenarios.learn: Free ‘helpful’ tools can harvest your data — check with IT before uploading any company information.

scenarios.title: Enable Content to View
scenarios.text: An attachment, “Invoice.docx,” opens to a banner: “Protected view — Enable Content to see this document.”

TRUST.text: You enable content. Hidden macro malware runs on your machine.

VERIFY.text: You weren’t expecting it, so you don’t enable anything and check the sender.

REPORT.text: You report it as phishing; the sender is blocked.

IGNORE.text: You close the file. Safe.

ESCALATE.text: IT examine the file in a safe environment.

scenarios.learn: ‘Enable content’ on an unexpected document can run hidden malware — when in doubt, report it.

scenarios.title: Left on the Train
scenarios.text: You realise your work laptop — logged in and full of client data — was left on the train.

TRUST.text: You wait and hope it turns up. The data is exposed if the wrong person finds it.

VERIFY.text: You retrace your steps and check lost property — but every minute counts.

REPORT.text: You tell IT immediately; they remote-lock and wipe it before anyone can access it.

IGNORE.text: You say nothing. The unsecured laptop becomes a breach.

ESCALATE.text: IT and your manager handle containment and any disclosure.

scenarios.best: REPORT (fast, so IT can wipe it)
scenarios.learn: Report lost devices immediately — a quick remote lock or wipe turns a disaster into a non-event.

scenarios.title: Too Much Information
scenarios.text: An auto-reply reads: “On holiday in Bali until the 20th. For payments contact Maria; for the CEO’s diary, Tom (+number).”

TRUST.text: The detailed reply stays on — it hands attackers a ready-made map for a payment scam.

VERIFY.text: You trim it to the bare minimum before turning it on.

REPORT.text: You suggest a company-wide auto-reply guideline.

IGNORE.text: The over-sharing reply stays live and the risk remains.

ESCALATE.text: A manager updates the team’s out-of-office standard.

scenarios.learn: Out-of-office replies can reveal names, dates and who approves money — keep them vague.

scenarios.title: From the New Auditor
scenarios.text: A polite caller claims to be a new external auditor and asks you to email a list of all staff names, emails and job titles ‘for the audit.’

TRUST.text: You send the list. It’s used to build a convincing mass-phishing campaign.

VERIFY.text: You confirm with your own finance/audit contact — there is no such auditor.

REPORT.text: You report the call; staff are warned of the pretext.

IGNORE.text: You don’t send anything. Safe.

ESCALATE.text: A manager verifies whether any audit is actually engaged.

scenarios.learn: ‘Officials’ who pressure you for staff data are a classic pretext — verify through a contact you already trust.

scenarios.title: Read Me Your Code
scenarios.text: Right after a login alert, you get a call: “Hi, your bank’s fraud team here. To cancel the suspicious login, read me the 6-digit code we just texted you.”

TRUST.text: You read the code aloud. That code was the key — the attacker completes their login.

VERIFY.text: You hang up and call the bank on the number on your card. It’s a scam.

REPORT.text: You report the call and freeze the account.

IGNORE.text: You hang up. Safe.

ESCALATE.text: You loop in your bank’s verified fraud line.

scenarios.learn: No real bank or service ever asks you to read back a one-time code — that code unlocks your account.

scenarios.title: Day-One Request
scenarios.text: It’s a new hire’s first morning. An email from ‘Facilities’ asks them to confirm their home address, ID number and bank details via a web form ‘to set up payroll.’

TRUST.text: They fill in the form. Their identity and bank details are phished.

VERIFY.text: They ask HR how payroll is really set up — through the official system, not a form.

REPORT.text: They flag it; the fake onboarding email is blocked.

IGNORE.text: Payroll setup stalls briefly, but no harm done.

ESCALATE.text: HR confirms the correct, secure process.

scenarios.learn: New hires are prime targets — confirm any ‘onboarding’ data request with HR directly, never via a random form.

roles.name: Receptionist / Front Desk
roles.description: The first point of contact for every visitor, call and delivery.
roles.ability: Gatekeeper — once per game, auto-resolve any physical/visitor scenario as VERIFY (prevented).

roles.name: HR Manager
roles.description: Holds the personal data of everyone in the company.
roles.targetedBy: Fake onboarding, data requests, payroll phishing.
roles.ability: Records Lock — once per game, cancel one Reputation loss from a data-leak outcome.

roles.name: Finance Officer
roles.description: Controls payments — the prize attackers want most.
roles.targetedBy: Invoice fraud, business-email compromise, deepfake transfer calls.
roles.ability: Dual Control — once per game, force VERIFY on a money scenario (no Funds lost that round).

roles.name: CEO / Executive
roles.description: High authority and a public profile — often the person attackers impersonate.
roles.targetedBy: Impersonation and deepfakes (as the faked person), targeted phishing.
roles.ability: Set the Tone — once per game, reduce the team’s Stress by 10.

roles.name: Intern / New Hire
roles.description: Eager to help and still learning what’s normal.
roles.targetedBy: Pressure tactics, onboarding scams, oversharing online.
roles.ability: Beginner’s Question — once per game, ask the facilitator one yes/no question before deciding.

roles.name: IT / Developer
roles.description: Keeps the systems running and is trusted with access.
roles.targetedBy: Vishing (‘IT support’), malicious files, shadow IT.
roles.ability: Patch It — once per game, turn one REPORT into a guaranteed ‘attack prevented.’

roles.name: Salesperson
roles.description: Always on the move, with lots of external contact and devices.
roles.targetedBy: Public Wi-Fi, lost devices, QR scams.
roles.ability: Road Warrior — once per game, ignore one Stress penalty.

events.title: IT Is Overloaded
events.text: Any REPORT this round takes effect next round instead of now.

events.title: The CEO Is on Vacation
events.text: You can’t verify with the CEO this round: +5 Stress on any CEO-related scenario.

events.title: Journalist Calls
events.text: If Reputation is below 60, lose 5 more Reputation. Otherwise, no effect.

events.title: Payday Rush
events.text: Finance is slammed — the next money scenario adds +5 Stress.

events.title: Audit Week
events.text: Good security calls this round earn +5 extra Security Score.

events.title: Holiday Scam Surge
events.text: Draw and resolve TWO scenario cards this round.

events.title: Phishing Simulation
events.text: Your own security team sends a test. Resolve the next scenario with no Funds penalty.

events.title: New-Starter Week
events.text: Every scenario this round targets the Intern / New Hire if possible.

events.title: Backups Verified
events.text: Reward: the next REPORT on a ransomware/malware scenario fully prevents it.

events.title: VIP Visit
events.text: Reputation is fragile — any TRUST mistake this round costs +5 extra Reputation.
